verses.text: “Mas agora se riem de mim os de menos idade do que eu.” O capítulo muda. Jó vinha recordando sua honra anterior (cap. 29); agora lamenta a desonra. Até os jovens agora zombam dele. O hebraico *sechoq* — “riso, escárnio” — nomeia o pior da descida social: não a perda em si, mas o desprezo que se segue.

No passado, os membros mais honrados da sociedade falavam bem de Jó ( **29.11** ); agora, os menos honrados zombavam dele ( **30.1** ) e cuspiam em seu rosto ( **30.10** ). Cf. **Sl 35.15** ; **69.12** ; **Mc 14.65** ; **15.17-20** .

No passado, os membros mais honrados da sociedade falavam bem de Jó ( **29.11** ); agora, os menos honrados zombavam dele ( **30.1** ) e cuspiam em seu rosto ( **30.10** ). Cf. **Sl 35.15** ; **69.12** ; **Mc 14.65** ; **15.17-20** .

30:4 mallows—rather, "salt-wort," which grows in deserts and is eaten as a salad by the poor [MAURER]. by the bushes—among the bushes. juniper—rather, a kind of broom, Spartium junceum [LINNÃ†US], still called in Arabia, as in the Hebrew of Job, retem, of which the bitter roots are eaten by the poor.

vivem em ravinas assustadoras, em cavernas e entre as rochas: A vida miserável dos zombadores de Jó assemelhava-se à situação de Israel sob a opressão midianita ( **Jz 6.2** ), a vida de Davi enquanto fugia de Saul ( **1Sm 22.1-2** ), e a vida de Elias enquanto aguardava a instrução do Senhor ( **1Rs 17.3-8** ).

vivem em ravinas assustadoras, em cavernas e entre as rochas: A vida miserável dos zombadores de Jó assemelhava-se à situação de Israel sob a opressão midianita ( **Jz 6.2** ), a vida de Davi enquanto fugia de Saul ( **1Sm 22.1-2** ), e a vida de Elias enquanto aguardava a instrução do Senhor ( **1Rs 17.3-8** ).

30:7 brayed—like the wild ass (Job 6:5 for food). The inarticulate tones of this uncivilized rabble are but little above those of the beast of the field. gathered together—rather, sprinkled here and there. Literally, "poured out," graphically picturing their disorderly mode of encampment, lying up and down behind the thorn bushes. nettles—or brambles [UMBREIT].

30:8 fools—that is, the impious and abandoned (1 Sam 25:25). base—nameless, low-born rabble. viler than, &c.—rather, they were driven or beaten out of the land. The Horites in Mount Seir (Gen 14:6 with which compare Gen 36:20-21; Deu 2:12, Deu 2:22) were probably the aborigines, driven out by the tribe to which Job's ancestors belonged; their name means troglodytÃ&brvbr, or "dwellers in caves." To these Job alludes here (Job 30:1-8, and Gen 24:4-8, which compare together).
Eles zombam de mim! Veja também **17.6** ; cp. **Sl 69.12** ; **Lm 3.14** , **63** .

não chegam perto de mim: Veja também **19.13-15** ; cp. **Sl 88.8** ; **Pv 19.7** ; **Mt 26.56** . • Cuspir no rosto de alguém era mostrar repulsa ou desprezo ( **Dt 25.9** ; **Is 50.6** ; **Mt 26.67** ; **27.30** ; **Mc 14.65** ).

30:11 He—that is, "God"; antithetical to "they"; English Version here follows the marginal reading (Keri). my cord—image from a bow unstrung; opposed to Job 29:20. The text (Chetib), "His cord" or "reins" is better; "yea, each lets loose his reins" [UMBREIT].

A série de imagens apresentadas aqui é tirada de um avanço militar contra uma cidade fortificada. Jó já havia usado essa imagem para o ataque de Deus contra ele ( **19.10-12** ). • A palavra traduzida como "brechas" pode se referir a rampas de cerco erguidas contra as muralhas de uma cidade.

30:16 And now my soul is poured out upon me,.... Either in prayer to God for help and deliverance; or rather he was dissolved as it were in floods of tears, because of his distress and anguish; or his spirits were sunk, his strength and courage failed, and his heart melted, and was poured out like water; yea, his soul was pouring out unto death, and he was, as he apprehended, near unto it; his body was so weakened and broken by diseases, that it was like a vessel full of holes, out of which the liquor runs away apace;

30:17 In the Hebrew, night is poetically personified, as in Job 3:3 : "night pierceth my bones (so that they fall) from me" (not as English Version, "in me"; see Job 30:30). sinews—so the Arabic, "veins," akin to the Hebrew; rather, "gnawers" (see on Job 30:3), namely, my gnawing pains never cease. Effects of elephantiasis.

Deus me agarra... pela garganta: O hebraico neste versículo é difícil de traduzir; pode significar que Jó sente como se estivesse em um estrangulamento e prestes a ser jogado na lama (cp. **30.19** ).
pó e cinzas: Antes, Jó estava sentado “entre as cinzas” em angústia ( **2.8** ); mais tarde, ele “se sentaria em pó e cinzas para mostrar... arrependimento” ( **42.6** ).
você não responde: Esta era a queixa frequente de Jó ( **9.16** ; **19.7** ; **23.2-9** ; **31.35** ), que Deus logo respondeu ( **38.1** ).
você não responde: Esta era a queixa frequente de Jó ( **9.16** ; **19.7** ; **23.2-9** ; **31.35** ), que Deus logo respondeu ( **38.1** ).
Jó se sentiu jogado no redemoinho e soprado como palha ou palhiço sem valor ( **9.17** ; **21.18** ; **27.21** ; **Sl 1.4** ; **Is 17.13** ).

30:23 For I know that thou wilt bring me to death,.... Quickly and by the present affliction upon him; he was assured, as he thought, that this was the view and design of God in this providence, under which he was to bring him to death and the grave; that he would never take off his hand till he had brought him to the dust of death, to that lifeless dust from whence he had his original; otherwise, that he would he brought thither, sooner or later, was no great masterpiece of knowledge;

30:24 Expressing Job's faith as to the state after death. Though one must go to the grave, yet He will no more afflict in the ruin of the body (so Hebrew for "grave") there, if one has cried to Him when being destroyed. The "stretching of His hand" to punish after death answers antithetically to the raising "the cry" of prayer in the second clause. MAURER gives another translation which accords with the scope of Job 30:24-31; if it be natural for one in affliction to ask aid, why should it be considered (by the friends) wrong in my case?
30:25 May I not be allowed to complain of my calamity, and beg relief, seeing that I myself sympathized with those "in trouble" (literally, "hard of day"; those who had a hard time of it).

30:26 When I looked for good,.... As he thought he might reasonably expect it, since he had shown such a sympathizing spirit to persons in trouble, and such pity and mercy to the poor: in the time of his prosperity, he looked for a continuance of the good things he enjoyed, and expected to have had them for many years to come, and to have died in the possession of them, Job 29:18; and even in his adversity, though he had received evil things at the hand of God, which he took patiently;

30:27 My bowels boiled, and rested not,.... All contained within him, his heart, lungs, and liver, in a literal sense, through a violent fever burning within him; or figuratively, being under great distress and trouble, by reason of his afflictions, outward and inward, see Jer 4:19; the days of affliction prevented me; came sooner upon him than he thought; he did not expect the evil days to come, and the years draw nigh in which he should have no pleasure, until he was more advanced in years, and the time of his dissolution was at hand;
Antes de seu teste começar, Jó era respeitado na praça pública ( **29.7-10** , **21-25** ); ele ajudava os necessitados ( **29.11-17** ).

Eu sou considerado: Jó pode ter estado expressando o que pensava de si mesmo, como os outros o viam, ou ambos. • Ao afirmar que era irmão dos chacais e companheiro dos avestruzes , Jó pode ter estado se descrevendo como em meio a um lamento ( **Mq 1.8** ). Os chacais eram associados à desolação ou ruína (veja **Sl 63.10** ; **Is 13.22** ; **34.13** ; **35.7** ; **Jr 9.11** ; **10.22** ; **49.33** ; **51.37** ; **Lm 5.18** ; **Ez 13.4** ; **Ml 1.3** ).
A pele de uma pessoa que vivia em circunstâncias confortáveis era protegida do sol e do vento; pele escura indicava declínio físico e social ( **Lm 4.8** ; **5.10** ).

30:31 organ—rather, "pipe" (Job 21:12). "My joy is turned into the voice of weeping" (Lam 5:15). These instruments are properly appropriated to joy (Isa 30:29, Isa 30:32), which makes their use now in sorrow the sadder by contrast. Next: Job Chapter 31
